title: MCP вҖ” Model Context Protocol
eli8:
## MCP вҖ” The USB-C for AI! рҹ”Ң

You know how USB-C is awesome because you can use the *same cable* to charge your phone, connect your laptop, plug in your headphones, and connect to a screen? Before USB-C, you needed a DIFFERENT cable for everything вҖ” so annoying! рҹҳӨ

Well, AI apps had the same problem. If you wanted your AI to:
- Read your files
- Search the web  
- Check your GitHub
- Look at your Slack messages

...someone had to build a *custom connector* for EVERY SINGLE tool. And if you had 10 different AI apps, you needed 10 Г— 4 = 40 custom connectors! That's a nightmare! рҹҳұ

### MCP Saves the Day! рҹҰё

**MCP** (Model Context Protocol) is like inventing USB-C for AI tools. Now:
- Tool makers build ONE MCP server (like making a USB-C port)
- AI apps become MCP clients (like making a USB-C device)
- They ALL connect to each other automatically!

So instead of 40 custom connectors, you just need everyone to support MCP. Simple!

### What Can MCP Servers Do? рҹӣ пёҸ

An MCP server can offer three types of things to AI apps:

- рҹ“ҡ **Resources**: Give the AI data to read (files, database rows, web pages)
- рҹ”§ **Tools**: Let the AI take actions (create a file, send a message, run a search)
- рҹ“қ **Prompts**: Pre-built conversation templates the AI can use

### Real Examples рҹҢҚ

There are now 500+ MCP servers built by the community!
- A **Filesystem MCP server** lets any AI read and write your local files
- A **GitHub MCP server** lets any AI create issues and pull requests
- A **PostgreSQL MCP server** lets any AI query your database
- A **Slack MCP server** lets any AI read and post messages

MCP makes the whole AI ecosystem work together like a superteam! рҹҡҖ
detail:
## Model Context Protocol (MCP)

**MCP** is an open protocol developed by Anthropic (released November 2024) that standardizes how AI applications connect to data sources and tools. It solves the NГ—M integration problem: without a standard, every AI app must build custom connectors to every tool, requiring NГ—M integrations for N apps and M tools. With MCP, both sides implement one standard and they all interconnect.

## Architecture

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ         MCP Host                в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (Claude Desktop, IDE, App)     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ                                 в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ MCP Client  в”Ӯв—„в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ protocol в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә MCP Server
в”Ӯ  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                в”Ӯ      (tool provider)
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

### MCP Host
The AI application that embeds an LLM вҖ” Claude Desktop, VS Code with an AI extension, a custom application. The host manages user interaction and LLM calls.

### MCP Client
A protocol handler embedded in the host. It manages connections to MCP servers, negotiates capabilities, and routes requests. One host can connect to multiple MCP servers simultaneously.

### MCP Server
A lightweight process that exposes capabilities over the MCP protocol. It runs locally (stdio) or remotely (HTTP+SSE). Any tool or data source can be wrapped as an MCP server.

## Transport Mechanisms

| Transport | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **stdio** | Local servers, same machine, subprocess communication |
| **HTTP + SSE** | Remote servers, cloud-hosted tools, streaming responses |

## Core Primitives

### Resources
Data sources the LLM can read (analogous to GET requests):
- File contents, database records, API responses
- Identified by URIs: `file:///path/to/file`, `postgres://db/table`
- Can be static or dynamic (subscriptions for live data)

### Tools
Actions the LLM can invoke (analogous to POST requests):
- Defined with a name, description, and JSON Schema for parameters
- The LLM decides when and how to call them
- Examples: `create_file`, `search_github`, `send_slack_message`

### Prompts
Pre-built conversation templates or workflows:
- Reusable interaction patterns exposed by the server
- Example: a Git MCP server might offer a `commit_message_helper` prompt

## Building an MCP Server (Python)

```python
from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.server.models import InitializationOptions
import mcp.types as types

server = Server("my-weather-server")

@server.list_tools()
async def handle_list_tools() -> list[types.Tool]:
    return [
        types.Tool(
            name="get_weather",
            description="Get current weather for a city",
            inputSchema={
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["city"]
            }
        )
    ]

@server.call_tool()
async def handle_call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict) -> list[types.TextContent]:
    if name == "get_weather":
        city = arguments["city"]
        weather = fetch_weather_api(city)  # your implementation
        return [types.TextContent(type="text", text=str(weather))]
```

Run over stdio:
```python
from mcp.server.stdio import stdio_server
async with stdio_server() as (read_stream, write_stream):
    await server.run(read_stream, write_stream, InitializationOptions(...))
```

## MCP vs. Function Calling

| Aspect | Function Calling | MCP |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Per-session, per-application | Reusable across any MCP host |
| **Discovery** | Defined in each API call | Server advertises capabilities |
| **Deployment** | Code in the app | Separate server process |
| **Reuse** | Must re-implement per app | Build once, use everywhere |

## Growing Ecosystem

As of 2025, the MCP ecosystem includes 500+ community servers:
- **Official**: filesystem, GitHub, Slack, Google Drive, PostgreSQL, web browser
- **Community**: Jira, Linear, Notion, Stripe, Twilio, AWS, Docker
- **IDE integration**: Claude Code, Cursor, Continue, VS Code

## Security Considerations

- Servers run as separate processes with their own permissions
- Tool call schemas must be validated to prevent injection
- Users should review what capabilities an MCP server exposes before connecting
- OAuth2 support for authenticated remote MCP servers

mcqs.question: What fundamental problem does MCP (Model Context Protocol) solve?
options: It reduces the cost of LLM API calls by caching responses
It standardizes AI-to-tool connections, eliminating the need for NГ—M custom integrations
It provides a protocol for AI models to communicate with each other
It defines a standard format for training data used in fine-tuning
mcqs.explanation: Without MCP, every AI app must build custom connectors to every tool, requiring NГ—M integrations. MCP provides a standard both sides implement, so any MCP host can connect to any MCP server automatically вҖ” the 'USB-C for AI tools' analogy.

mcqs.question: What are the three core primitives that an MCP server can expose?
options: Models, Datasets, and Evaluations
Inputs, Outputs, and Transforms
Resources, Tools, and Prompts
Functions, Classes, and Modules
mcqs.explanation: MCP servers expose three primitives: Resources (data the LLM can read, like files or database records), Tools (actions the LLM can invoke, like creating a file or sending a message), and Prompts (pre-built conversation templates or workflows).

mcqs.question: What transport mechanism does a local MCP server running as a subprocess typically use?
options: WebSockets
gRPC
stdio (standard input/output)
HTTP polling
mcqs.explanation: Local MCP servers communicate via stdio вҖ” standard input and output streams. The MCP host spawns the server as a subprocess and communicates by writing to its stdin and reading from its stdout. Remote servers use HTTP + SSE.

mcqs.question: How does MCP differ from function calling (tool use) in LLM APIs?
options: MCP tools can only read data; function calling can also write data
Function calling is more secure because tools are defined in code, not in a server
MCP tools are reusable across any MCP-compatible app; function calling tools must be re-implemented per application
MCP requires the LLM to be fine-tuned on tool descriptions; function calling does not
mcqs.explanation: The key difference is reusability. Function calling defines tools in each API call and app, requiring reimplementation everywhere. MCP servers are built once and work with any MCP-compatible host вҖ” Claude Desktop, VS Code, custom apps вҖ” without any additional integration work.

mcqs.question: In the MCP architecture, what is the role of the MCP client?
options: It is the external tool or data source that the AI app connects to
It is the LLM that generates responses using tool outputs
It is a protocol handler embedded in the host that manages connections to MCP servers
It is the end user who interacts with the AI application
mcqs.explanation: The MCP client is a protocol handler embedded inside the MCP host (the AI application). It manages connections to one or more MCP servers, negotiates capabilities, and routes tool/resource/prompt requests between the LLM and the appropriate server.

mcqs.question: What is an MCP 'Resource' analogous to in web API terms?
options: A POST request that creates or modifies data
A WebSocket connection for streaming data
A GET request that retrieves data without side effects
A DELETE request that removes data
mcqs.explanation: MCP Resources represent data sources the LLM can read вҖ” analogous to GET requests. They are identified by URIs and return content. Tools are the write/action equivalent (analogous to POST requests).

mcqs.question: Which Python decorator in the MCP SDK defines which tools a server exposes to clients?
options: @server.register_tools()
@server.list_tools()
@server.expose_tools()
@server.tool_definitions()
mcqs.explanation: In the MCP Python SDK, the @server.list_tools() decorator marks the function that returns the list of Tool objects the server exposes. When a client connects, it calls this to discover what the server can do.

flashcards.back: An open protocol by Anthropic (2024) standardizing how AI applications connect to tools and data sources. Solves the NГ—M integration problem by providing a universal 'USB-C for AI tools' standard.
A process that exposes capabilities (Resources, Tools, Prompts) to AI apps over the MCP protocol. Can run locally via stdio or remotely via HTTP+SSE. Build once, works with any MCP host.
The AI application embedding an LLM that connects to MCP servers (e.g., Claude Desktop, VS Code with AI extension, a custom app). Manages user interaction and LLM calls.
A protocol handler embedded in an MCP host that manages connections to MCP servers, negotiates capabilities, and routes requests. One host can connect to multiple servers simultaneously.
A data source an LLM can read via MCP. Analogous to a GET request. Identified by URI (e.g., file:///path, postgres://db/table). Examples: file contents, database rows, API responses.
An action an LLM can invoke via MCP. Analogous to a POST request. Defined with a name, description, and JSON Schema for parameters. Examples: create_file, search_github, send_slack_message.
A pre-built conversation template or workflow exposed by an MCP server. Reusable interaction patterns the LLM can request and use, e.g., a commit_message_helper prompt from a Git server.
The transport mechanism for local MCP servers: the host spawns the server as a subprocess and communicates via standard input/output streams. Simple, secure, and no network required.
Server-Sent Events over HTTP: the transport for remote MCP servers. Enables streaming responses and works across network boundaries. Used when the MCP server is cloud-hosted.

videos.title: MCP Explained вҖ” Model Context Protocol
videos.description: A clear explainer on what MCP is, why Anthropic created it, how the host-client-server architecture works, and why it matters for the AI ecosystem.

videos.title: Building Your First MCP Server вҖ” Tutorial
videos.description: Hands-on walkthrough of building an MCP server with the Python SDK, defining Resources and Tools, and connecting it to Claude Desktop.
